presentation_title: The New Face of Colorectal Cancer: What Every Surgeon and Endoscopist Should Know
presenter: Richard L. Whelan, MD (Mount Sinai West Hospital, New York, NY)
conference: A View Through the Scope – Past, Present & Future – 2019
chair: Peter W. Marcello, MD; Co-Chair: James Ellsmere, MD
reasoning: This is an instructional/educational presentation delivered at a surgical conference. It contains narrated lecture slides, educational content with overlays, and demonstration materials. It is NOT a performance video of a trainee executing an FLS task. Therefore, it does not receive an FLS task score.

teaching_content.video_purpose: Educational lecture on endoscopy simulation and training for surgeons and endoscopists preparing for colorectal cancer management
demonstrated_topics: Simulation: Basic Concept – why simulation-based training is superior to patient-based learning
Importance of endoscopic skills in general surgery – diagnostic and therapeutic applications
American Board of Surgery Training Mandate for Endoscopy – 5-level certification requirements, FES certification, case minimums
Dedicated training time requirements across all training models
First skill to be learned: control of scope and insertion – scope anatomy, deflection wheels, suction/air/water buttons, image capture, scope tip anatomy
Endoscopy Simulators – Simbionix, CAE/Endo VR, Accutouch platforms
Torso Trainers – physical models with adjustable tethering points for variable difficulty
Ex-vivo porcine and bovine bowel models for interventional training – realistic but resource-intensive
Materials and equipment required for endoscopic training – comprehensive list provided
Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection (ESD) Training – tissue cutting mechanics, scope positioning, hot knife control
ESD in Japan – stomach-focused training progression (40-60 cases), advancement to colon after proficiency
Figure-tracing inanimate model for knife tip control – hollow plastic tube colon model, modified sclerotherapy catheter pen, dye-colored saline ink
Skill acquisition metrics – trainee performance graph showing learning curve across 4 pattern types over 8 training sessions
ESD Training Model: Bovine Large Bowel – 30 cases per surgeon, repetition-based skill development

Summary points: modern surgeon must be endoscopist, endoscopy now mandated, FES accessible, multiple training platforms available, ex-vivo models more realistic for advanced skills, dedicated time carving essential
key_concepts: Learning on patients is suboptimal – simulation provides stress-free, repetitive practice
Simulation models allow independent skill acquisition without clinical volume constraints
Multiple simulation modalities exist with varying fidelity and resource requirements
Scope control and insertion is foundational skill – must master before advanced procedures
ESD is exceedingly difficult skill requiring dedicated training with appropriate models
Progression from computer-based to ex-vivo models reflects increasing complexity and realism
Regular repetition on appropriate models drives competency acquisition
Institutional programs must dedicate protected training time for competency development
FES Certification (Fundamentals of Endoscopic Surgery) required by American Board of Surgery
verbal_cues: Richard L. Whelan, MD explicitly states simulation rationale and training requirements
Presenter discusses American Board of Surgery mandates and certification levels
Educational slides clearly label each training modality and its applications
Graph data shows trainee performance improvement over repeated sessions
Summary slide reinforces key takeaways for surgical training programs
educational_modalities_shown: Visual demonstrations of training equipment (Simbionix, CAE/Endo VR, Accutouch, Torso Trainers)
Endoscopic video clips showing clinical anatomy and procedural landmarks
Schematic diagrams of training models (figure-tracing model cross-section)
Clinical images of ex-vivo tissue training models
common_errors_addressed: Learning fundamentals on real patients – ineffective and dangerous approach
Insufficient scope control training before advanced endoscopic procedures
Inadequate repetition on models to achieve proficiency
Failure to carve protected training time in clinical schedules
Adoption of unrealistic training models that do not translate to clinical skill
teaching_content.target_audience: Surgical residents, surgical educators, program directors, general surgeons transitioning to endoscopy, endoscopists

frame_summary: Presentation consists of 20+ slides covering simulation principles, training mandates, equipment options, procedural techniques, and skill acquisition data. Each slide contains educational content with supporting visuals, photographs, diagrams, and clinical images. No live task performance is present.
fls_scoring_comment: This video does not contain an FLS task performance and therefore receives no FLS score. It is educational content supporting the broader context of surgical skills training.
